summary:
Sorghum (Sorghum bicolor) is an African cereal crop of global importance, domesticated in northeastern Africa — most likely in the region of modern Sudan, Chad, and Ethiopia — during the period approximately 3,000–1,000 BCE according to the best current archaeological and genetic evidence, substantially later than the period of this era. However, the wild progenitor grasses that would eventually yield domestic sorghum were present across the savanna and Sahel zones of northeastern Africa throughout the early Holocene, exploited by hunter-gatherer communities during the period when the [[event:17|African Humid Period]] made these environments far more productive than today.

During 10,000–8,000 BCE, communities along the Nile Valley in Sudan and in the broader savanna zones of the eastern Sahara were intensively exploiting wild grasses including wild sorghum relatives as part of a broad-spectrum hunter-gatherer economy. The exploitation of wild grasses in this region during the Green Sahara period represents the cultural and subsistence background from which African cereal domestication would eventually emerge. Sorghum's eventual domestication gave sub-Saharan Africa one of its most important food crops, adapted to the hot, dry conditions of the African interior and resistant to drought and poor soil conditions that challenge other cereals.
